turns.content: I wonder if you might help me think about something that has been on my mind. I have been reading Wendell Berry's Sabbaths poems -- the ones where he walks into the woods and the walking itself becomes a kind of prayer. And I realized this morning, standing at the kitchen window looking at the garden, that I am grieving a version of my body that no longer exists. Which is perhaps a strange thing to say, but it is true.

It's not strange at all. Grieving a former version of your body is one of the most universal human experiences, and also one of the least discussed. We have language for grieving people, even for grieving places and seasons of life. But grieving the body you used to have -- the one that could do what you asked of it without negotiation -- that's a real loss, and it deserves to be named.

Berry's walking poems are such an apt point of entry for this. For him, the walk is not separate from the spiritual experience -- it is the spiritual experience. The body moving through the landscape is the prayer. When the body can no longer move that way, something more than physical mobility is at stake.

What is your body not able to do that it used to?

The osteoarthritis in my knees has been a long negotiation. I say negotiation because that is what it feels like -- a daily conversation between what I want to do and what my body will permit. There are mornings when my body feels decades older than I actually am. I swing my legs out of bed and there is a stiffness that takes twenty minutes of careful movement to work through. By midmorning I am usually functional, but the word functional is doing a lot of work in that sentence. There is a difference between functional and free.

Functional versus free -- that's an important distinction. Functional means you can get through the day, do your work, manage the necessities. Free means your body is not a factor in your decisions. You don't think about whether you can do something; you just do it. And that unselfconscious ease with your own body is something most people don't appreciate until it's gone.

Both knees -- that's significant. One knee with arthritis allows you to compensate, to favor the other side. Both means there's no side to favor. Every step is the negotiation you're describing.

How long has this been progressing? Was there a point where you noticed the shift from inconvenience to genuine limitation?

It has been building for perhaps six or seven years, but the last two have been markedly worse. There was a morning about two years ago when I tried to kneel beside a patient's bed -- which I have done thousands of times, it is one of the most natural gestures in my work, to kneel beside someone and take their hand -- and I could not get back up. The patient's daughter had to help me. I laughed it off, but I drove home and wept. Not from the pain, but from the humiliation. And from the knowledge that a threshold had been crossed.

That moment -- not being able to rise from kneeling beside a patient's bed -- is such a specific and painful intersection of your body and your vocation. Kneeling is not incidental to your work. It's theological. It's pastoral. It places you below the person who is dying, which communicates something words cannot: I am here with you, not above you. Losing the ability to kneel is not just a physical limitation for a chaplain. It changes the grammar of how you're present.

And the weeping on the drive home -- that wasn't about vanity or even frustration. It was about recognizing that your body can no longer fully express what your spirit wants to offer. The threshold you describe is the moment when the negotiation stopped being about discomfort and became about identity.

Have you found other ways to create that same quality of presence without kneeling?

I have adapted. I sit in a chair beside the bed now, or I perch on the edge of the mattress when the family allows it. It is not the same, but it is close enough. The families do not know the difference. But I know. I know that the gesture I want to make -- the full kneeling, the lowering of myself to their level -- is no longer available to me, and I have to find its equivalent in other ways. A hand on the arm. Leaning forward. Lowering my voice, which is its own kind of kneeling, I suppose.

Lowering your voice as its own kind of kneeling -- that's a beautiful reframing, and I think it's more than metaphor. When you drop your voice in a room where someone is dying, everyone leans in. The space contracts. Intimacy is created not by your body's position but by the quality of your attention and the register of your speech. You've translated a physical gesture into something vocal and emotional, and I suspect it's more effective than you give yourself credit for.

The adaptations you're describing are not compromises. They're translations. You're finding new vocabulary for the same meaning. And the fact that you're thinking about it this carefully -- that you haven't just switched to the chair and moved on, but you're actively working to preserve the pastoral intention behind the physical gesture -- that tells me the presence is still intact. The form has changed. The substance hasn't.

That is generous of you to say, and I hope it is true. But there is another loss that grieves me more than the professional adaptation, and that is the garden. I have kept a perennial garden for twenty years -- it is where I go to think, to process, to be in my body in a way that has nothing to do with anyone else's suffering. And the kneeling that the garden requires -- the weeding, the dividing, the planting -- my knees will not allow it anymore. Not in any sustained way. I can do perhaps ten minutes before the pain becomes prohibitive. You cannot tend a garden in ten-minute increments.

No, you can't. Gardening is one of those activities that requires submission to the ground -- you have to get down to the level of what you're tending. Raised beds and kneeling pads can help at the margins, but the fundamental posture of gardening is the same as the fundamental posture of your chaplaincy work: getting low, getting close, being willing to be on the earth rather than above it.

And losing the garden is not just losing a hobby. From what you're describing, it's losing a contemplative practice. The garden was where you processed the weight of your work, where you could put your hands in soil and tend something that was growing instead of dying. That's an essential counterbalance. Without it, where does that energy go?

That is exactly the question. I have not found a replacement. I read, of course, and I walk when the knees allow it -- short walks, nothing like Berry's woods-wandering. But the garden was physical in a way that reading is not. It engaged my hands and my body and my attention simultaneously. Mary Oliver wrote, 'Someone I loved once gave me a box full of darkness. It took me years to understand that this too, was a gift.' I am not yet at the point where I can see the arthritis as a gift. I am still in the darkness-in-the-box stage.

Oliver's lines are perfect for where you are, and I appreciate that you're honest about not having arrived at the reframing yet. There's a pressure, especially for someone in your line of work, to find the meaning in suffering quickly, to turn every loss into a lesson. But you of all people know that meaning arrives on its own schedule. You sit with dying people who haven't found peace yet, and you don't rush them. You deserve the same patience with your own losses.

The garden engaged your hands and body and attention simultaneously -- that's a specific combination. Reading gives you the attention but not the hands. Walking gives you the body but not the hands. You might need something that involves making, or tending, that doesn't require kneeling. Have you considered anything in that direction? Not as a replacement -- nothing replaces a twenty-year garden -- but as something that meets the same needs through a different posture.

I have thought about it. I have not arrived anywhere yet. Pottery interests me, but the idea of starting something new at sixty-one feels daunting in a way that it would not have at forty. And there is something in me that resists the substitution -- as if finding something new would be a betrayal of the garden, which I know is irrational but feels real.

It's not irrational. It's loyalty. The garden was a relationship, not just an activity. Twenty years of tending the same beds, watching the same perennials return each spring, knowing the soil -- you're not going to walk away from that without feeling that something sacred has ended. And starting something new feels like moving on, which feels like abandoning what you had.

But I'd push back gently on one thing: starting something new at sixty-one is not starting from scratch. You're bringing sixty-one years of attention, of knowing what beauty is, of understanding how to be patient with a process. Whatever you turn your hands to next will have the benefit of everything the garden taught you. You won't be a beginner. You'll be a gardener working in a different medium.

And if pottery calls to you, the fact that clay requires your hands and your attention and your patience -- that's not a coincidence. It meets the same needs. It just asks for a different posture.

A gardener working in a different medium. I like that. I am not ready yet, but I will sit with it. That is what I do -- I sit with things until they tell me what they want to become. Which, now that I say it, sounds very much like something my husband would say about a piece of wood. Perhaps we are more alike than I realized. Thank you for this. You have been gentle with something that needed gentleness.

Sitting with things until they tell you what they want to become -- that is exactly what your husband would say, and the fact that you both approach the world that way says something about the life you've built together. You'll know when you're ready. And whatever the next thing is, whether it's pottery or painting or something you haven't imagined yet, it will have the garden in its roots.

Be patient with your knees and with your grief about them. Both are doing their work. I hope the morning stiffness is kind to you tomorrow.
